section.title: The Obedient Servant
section.sectionRationale: This is the third of Isaiah's Servant Songs, and it turns a corner. Israel is confronted with a hard truth, she was not abandoned, she sold herself into exile through her own sin. Against that failure stands the Servant, whose ear is open, whose face is set like flint, and who is struck and spat upon yet never turns back. It is the quiet hinge before the fourth Song, the summit of Isaiah 52:13 to 53:12.
section.thesis: Israel's exile was self-inflicted, not evidence of a failed or forgetful God; and where Israel failed to listen, the Servant listens perfectly, offering His back to those who strike Him rather than turning away, so that His obedience becomes the ground of the salvation Israel could never earn.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the divorce that never happened; the Servant's open ear; the Servant vindicated though shamed; and a closing call to trust the LORD rather than one's own firebrands.

units.label: The divorce that never happened
units.note: Israel sold herself; God did not abandon her

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “Where is your mother’s certificate of divorce with which I sent her away? Or to which of My creditors did I sell you? Look, you were sold for your iniquities, and for your transgressions your mother was sent away.

Why was no one there when I arrived? Why did no one answer when I called? Is My hand too short to {{gaal|redeem}} you? Or do I lack the strength to deliver you? Behold, My rebuke dries up the sea; I turn the rivers into a desert; the fish rot for lack of water and die of thirst.
I clothe the heavens in black and make sackcloth their covering.”
The Lord GOD has given Me the tongue of {{limmud|discipleship}}, to sustain the weary with a word. He awakens Me morning by morning; He awakens My ear to listen as a disciple.
The Lord GOD has opened My ears, and I have not been rebellious, nor have I turned back.
I offered My back to those who struck Me, and My cheeks to those who tore out My beard. I did not hide My face from scorn and spittle.
Because the Lord GOD helps Me, I have not been disgraced; therefore I have set My face like {{chalamish|flint}}, and I know that I will not be put to shame.
The One who vindicates Me is near. Who will dare to contend with Me? Let us confront each other! Who has a case against Me? Let him approach Me!
Surely the Lord GOD helps Me. Who is there to condemn Me? See, they will all wear out like a garment; the moths will devour them.

units.label: A call to trust, a warning to self-reliance
units.note: lean on God's name, not your own firebrands

verses.text: Who among you fears the LORD and obeys the voice of His Servant? Who among you walks in darkness and has no light? Let him trust in the name of the LORD; let him lean on his God.

Behold, all you who kindle a fire, who array yourselves with firebrands, walk in the light of your fire and of the firebrands you have lit! This is what you will receive from My hand: You will lie down in a place of torment.

gaal.gloss: To redeem, to act as kinsman-deliverer for one who cannot free themselves. God asks whether His hand is too short to redeem, a rhetorical denial; the failure is never His power, it is Israel's sin. The word anchors the whole Servant section, since it is the Servant who will accomplish the redemption Israel could not achieve for herself.

limmud.gloss: One taught, instructed, a disciple. The Servant is given the tongue of the taught, so that His speech to the weary flows from an ear that is opened morning by morning to listen. It pictures a life of daily, unhurried attentiveness to the Father before there is a word for anyone else.

chalamish.gloss: Flint, hard rock. To set the face like flint is to fix oneself with unmovable resolve toward a course already known to bring suffering. Luke uses the same picture in Greek when Jesus set His face to go to Jerusalem, Luke 9:51, deliberately walking toward the cross.

basic.context: This is the third of four Servant Songs in Isaiah, the poems that describe a mysterious figure who will accomplish God's rescue. Here Israel is told plainly that her exile is not God's failure or forgetfulness, she sold herself through sin. Against that backdrop stands the Servant, whose ear is open to God and who absorbs abuse without retaliating or running.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter has two halves that interpret each other: Israel's self-inflicted judgment, and the Servant's innocent suffering. Israel deserved what happened to her; the Servant did not deserve what happens to Him, yet He accepts it anyway. That contrast, deserved suffering versus willing, undeserved suffering, is the theological engine that drives toward Isaiah 53.
basic.originalLanguages: God asks if His hand is too short to ga'al, redeem, His people, a flat no. The Servant is given the tongue of limmud, one taught, so He can sustain the weary with a word shaped by daily listening. And He sets His face like challamish, flint, immovable toward the suffering ahead. Every word points to a will that is settled, obedient, and unshaken.
basic.typology: The Servant's opened ear, struck back, and set face all foreshadow the passion of Christ, who was flogged, spat upon, and mocked, and who set His face to go to Jerusalem knowing what awaited Him there. His refusal to hide His face from shame anticipates Gethsemane and Golgotha.
basic.christ: The gospels describe Jesus struck, spat upon, and His beard reportedly plucked in mockery, matching verse 6 almost word for word, and Luke 9:51 echoes the flint-set face of verse 7. Jesus is the true Servant who listens perfectly to the Father and does not turn back, even to the cross, trusting the One who vindicates Him.
basic.god: God is not a forgetful or defeated deity; Israel's exile was just judgment for real sin, not evidence that His arm had grown short. Yet the same God who judges also raises up a Servant who absorbs judgment willingly, showing that His justice and His mercy are never in tension, they meet in the Servant.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 9:51 shows Jesus setting His face toward Jerusalem. Matthew 26:67 and 27:26 describe Him struck and spat upon. Luke 22:63-65 records the mockery. Romans 8:33-34 echoes the courtroom language of verse 8, who shall bring a charge against God's elect.
basic.oneStory: Where Israel's story is one of self-inflicted exile, the Servant's story is one of willing, undeserved suffering offered in trust. That obedient trust, carried all the way to the cross by Jesus, becomes the ground on which every other failed story can be forgiven and remade.

context.literary: The third of Isaiah's four Servant Songs (the others in 42:1-9, 49:1-13, and 52:13-53:12), framed by a courtroom scene against Israel's own complaint that God has abandoned her, followed by the Servant's first-person testimony of obedient suffering.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles in Babylon tempted to think God had divorced them for good or lacked the power to save; Isaiah answers that the fault lies entirely with Israel's sin, while quietly introducing the one whose obedience will secure what Israel's disobedience forfeited.
context.canonical: This Song sits between the second Servant Song, where the Servant is a light to the nations, and the fourth, the Suffering Servant of chapter 53, deepening the picture from mission to suffering before the full weight of substitutionary atonement is unveiled.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct Israel's despairing accusation that God had failed or forgotten her, to lay the blame for exile squarely on her own sin, and to introduce a Servant whose obedient, undeserved suffering stands in deliberate contrast to Israel's deserved judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes both Israel's guilt and the Servant's obedience; it prescribes trust in the LORD's name rather than in self-made light, verse 10 being a direct call to faith rather than mere narration.

debates.question: Is the Servant in this Song Israel or an individual?

views.label: Corporate Israel, at her best
views.case: Earlier Servant Songs sometimes call Israel God's servant, so some read this as the nation's ideal vocation to trust and obey where the actual nation failed.

views.label: An individual, distinct from the nation He serves
views.case: This Servant is struck, mocked, and vindicated in ways the New Testament applies directly and repeatedly to Jesus, and He is clearly distinguished from the disobedient Israel addressed in verses 1 to 3.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Israel could not fulfill this obedience herself; the New Testament identifies the true, obedient Servant as Jesus, who succeeds exactly where Israel and all humanity fail.

study.originalLanguages: Ga'al, redeem, frames God's rhetorical denial that His arm has grown short. Limmud, one taught, describes the Servant's daily-opened ear and disciplined tongue. Challamish, flint, pictures a face set with unbending resolve. Together the words trace a Servant shaped for endurance: taught, listening, and immovable.

typology.type: The Servant's struck back and plucked beard, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: The scourging and mockery of Jesus before His crucifixion, Matthew 26:67, 27:26.

typology.type: The Servant's face set like flint, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus setting His face to go to Jerusalem, Luke 9:51, walking deliberately toward the cross.

typology.type: The Servant's confidence in vindication, verses 8-9
typology.fulfillment: Christ's vindication in the resurrection, and Paul's echo of this courtroom language in Romans 8:33-34.

study.christ: Jesus is the Servant who listens perfectly where Israel would not, who offers His back to those who strike Him and does not hide His face from scorn and spittle, exactly as the Gospels record of His arrest and trial. He sets His face like flint toward Jerusalem, knowing the cross awaits, and trusts the Father who vindicates Him, first at the empty tomb and finally at His return. His obedience is not merely an example; it is the obedience that accomplishes the redemption Israel's sin had forfeited.
study.god: God is just: Israel's exile is the real consequence of real sin, not divine failure or forgetfulness. God is also faithful to raise up, in the very generation that has failed Him, a Servant who will not fail, showing that His redemptive purposes are never finally dependent on human obedience but are carried by His own provision.

crossReferences.note: Jesus sets His face to go to Jerusalem, echoing the Servant's face like flint.
Jesus is struck, mocked, and scourged, fulfilling the Servant's willing exposure to abuse.
Who shall bring a charge, who shall condemn, echoes the Servant's confidence that His vindicator is near.
Christ did not revile in return when reviled, the pattern of the Servant who did not hide His face from shame.

study.oneStory: Israel's exile exposes what happens when God's people will not listen; the Servant's obedience shows what happens when someone finally does, all the way to being struck and shamed. That obedient Servant is Jesus, and His unbending resolve toward the cross is the reason anyone walking in darkness can lean on the name of the LORD and not be put to shame.

quiz.q: According to Isaiah 50:1-3, why was Israel sent into exile?

options.text: Because of her own iniquities and transgressions, not because God's power or memory had failed.
options.note: Verse 1 states plainly that Israel was sold for her own sin.

options.text: Because God's hand had grown too short to redeem her.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly denies this; God's power was never the problem.

options.text: Because God had permanently divorced her with no way back.
options.note: The chapter uses a divorce metaphor, but verse 1's rhetorical question shows no such certificate exists, so this overstates the case.

options.text: Because of a famine that God could not prevent.
options.note: The text names sin, not natural disaster, as the cause.

quiz.q: What marks the Servant's response to being struck and mocked in verses 5-6?

options.text: He does not rebel or turn back, and does not hide His face from scorn and spittle.
He calls down fire on His attackers.
options.note: The opposite; He submits rather than retaliates.

options.text: He flees before the abuse can happen.
options.note: He offers His back and cheeks; He does not flee.

options.text: He remains silent about God's help throughout.
options.note: He is largely silent under abuse, but He does speak of God's help vindicating Him.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the Servant 'set His face like flint'?

options.text: He fixed Himself with unmovable resolve to face the suffering ahead.
options.note: This is the meaning Luke 9:51 applies directly to Jesus going to Jerusalem.

options.text: His face literally turned to stone.
options.note: It is a figure of resolve, not a literal transformation.

options.text: He hardened His heart against God.
options.note: This inverts the image; His resolve is settled obedience toward God, not hardness against Him, though it correctly senses unbending resolve.

options.text: He refused to speak to anyone again.
options.note: The image is about resolve to endure suffering, not about silence toward people generally.

quiz.q: What is the closing call of verses 10-11 to those who fear the LORD but walk in darkness?

options.text: Trust in the name of the LORD and lean on God, rather than kindling your own firebrands.
options.note: Verse 10 calls for trust; verse 11 warns against self-made light that ends in torment.

options.text: Light your own fire so you are never without comfort.
options.note: Verse 11 warns this leads to a place of torment, not comfort.

options.text: Wait until the darkness lifts on its own before trusting God.
options.note: The call is to trust God precisely while still walking in darkness.

options.text: Obey the Servant's voice, since He alone can be fully trusted.
options.note: Close, and verse 10 does link fearing the LORD with obeying His Servant's voice, but the explicit command given is to trust and lean on God's name.

questions: Israel blamed God for her exile, but the text says she sold herself through sin. Where might you be tempted to blame God for consequences that are really the fruit of your own choices?
The Servant offered His back to those who struck Him rather than retaliating or hiding. Where is God asking you to absorb wrong rather than repay it, trusting Him to vindicate you?
The Servant set His face like flint toward suffering He knew was coming. What is one hard, right thing you are avoiding that requires that same settled resolve?

Verse 10 asks who among those in darkness will trust in the name of the LORD rather than lighting their own fire. What firebrands, plans, comforts, or self-reliance, are you tempted to kindle instead of trusting God?
Jesus is the Servant who listened perfectly where we do not. How does knowing His obedience, not yours, is what secures your redemption change the way you carry your own failures this week?

translationNotes.note: Certificate of divorce renders a specific legal formula; the rhetorical question assumes no such document exists, God has not formally cast Israel off.
Tongue of discipleship, limmudim, can also be rendered tongue of the learned or the taught; it emphasizes a speech shaped by daily instruction rather than natural eloquence.
Firebrands renders a term for sparks or burning arrows; the picture is of people who manufacture their own light and warmth apart from God, only to be consumed by the very fire they lit.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 50:1-11; no em dashes present in this passage.
